data.title: CEF Weekly Review: Tender Offers Keep Generating Alpha
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund ("CEF") market activity from both the bottom-up – highlighting individual fund news and events – as well as the top-down – providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the second week of February. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company ("BDC") as well as the preferreds/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

Most CEF sectors were up on the week, buoyed by tighter credit spreads and higher stocks. Month-to-date, all sectors are up with EM Equity and Preferreds in the lead.

Discount action remains strong on the fixed-income side in particular.

Market Themes

The Virtus Total Return Fund (ZTR) announced preliminary results of its tender offer where it bought back 10% of outstanding shares at a 2% discount or a price of $6.4386 (stock closed at $5.81 at the end of the week or at a 12% discount).

The pro-ration factor is a very high 31%, resulting in a 6% annualized alpha - the highest in our population of "interval CEFs" or CEFs that offer periodic buybacks. ZTR is a particularly good case given its very generous 10% buy-back amount and a 2% discount.

The fund has a hybrid allocation profile. Its discount is relatively wide in the context of its sector, offering some margin of safety.

Other examples of "interval CEFs" are the Muni fund EIM and a number of BlackRock equity CEFs such as CII, BME and BCX.

Market Commentary

Multi-sector credit CEF Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund (ARDC) cut its distribution by 4.3% to $0.1125 from $0.1175. The most recent shareholder report shows net investment income of $0.1283. Recall that the fund has very favorable interest expense as about half of its financing comes from term preferreds with a weighted average coupon of just 2.8%. That has allowed it to generate pretty efficient net income and partly explains its outperformance in the sector.

However, because its floating-rate assets are less than its floating-rate liabilities, its net income will move lower in the face of lower rates. That should be reflected in the next shareholder report. Our High Income Portfolio held a sizable position in the fund until it re-rated higher. It’s one to keep an eye on in case we see a market downdraft.

Stance And Takeaways

This week we rotated between two of Nuveen's unleveraged tax-exempt CEFs, specifically, from the Municipal Income Fund (NMI) to the Select Maturities Municipal Fund (NIM). The valuation of NIM is much more attractive than that of its counterpart. NIM is a good lower-beta option in the sector due to its lack of leverage and modest duration. Moreover, unleveraged CEFs are generating very efficient investment income due to their discounts and lack of leverage (which is still relatively expensive).

data.title: Allspring Absolute Return Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Global central banks continued cutting rates in the fourth quarter with the Federal Reserve (Fed) determined to further support economic growth. We did see some divergence on the international side, though, with the European Central Bank continuing to cut at every meeting, while the Bank of England was more careful as inflation remained sticky. The Chinese central bank and government provided more stimulus as well that supported emerging markets in the short term, while Japan held off with rate hikes in the fourth quarter as the yen started to strengthen. Toward the end of the quarter, we saw some stabilization in inflation at slightly higher levels as the U.S. economy seems to have reaccelerated and benefited from the hopes of tax cuts and rate cuts.

Over the quarter, the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) was up 2.41% with the tech-heavy Nasdaq up nearly 6%. A strong rebound in technology stocks—particularly Tesla (TSLA) after the U.S. elections—led to U.S. equities outperforming European and Asian equities. Japanese equities performed strongly, benefiting from a more cautious Bank of Japan and a weaker Japanese yen. On the bond side, we could observe the opposite effect after the U.S. elections. A strong rise in real yields close to 2% pushed the nominal U.S. 10-year Treasury (US10Y) yield up by nearly 100 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%), from 3.6% to 4.6%. International bonds performed well as the outlook for weaker growth and U.S. trade tariffs affected investor sentiment. The U.S. dollar (USDOLLAR,DXY) started to rise again, benefiting from a slightly more hawkish Fed at the end of the year, while European central banks are expected to still cut significantly in 2025. U.S. growth is likely to continue to outperform other regions, and that also helped the dollar gain. Commodities were slightly down over the quarter, driven by oversupply fears and weaker global demand, while gold was down 1%, though it remained close to its all-time high.

Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance maybe lower or higher than the performance data quoted and assumes the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current month-end performance is available at the fund’s website, allspringglobal.com. Institutional Class shares are sold without a front-end sales charge or contingent deferred sales charge.

The fund’s gross expense ratio is 0.87%. The fund’s net expense ratio is 0.82%. The manager has contractually committed, through August 31, 2025, to waive fees and/or reimburse expenses to the extent necessary to cap the fund’s total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers at 0.33% for the Institutional Class. Brokerage commissions, stamp duty fees, interest, taxes, acquired fund fees and expenses, and extraordinary expenses are excluded from the expense cap. Prior to or after the commitment expiration date, the cap may be increased or the commitment to maintain the cap may be terminated only with the approval of the Board of Trustees. Without this cap, the fund’s returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning: Absolute Return Fund Portfolio Allocations (%) at Quarter End

Overall Asset Allocation

Our overall equity exposure was unchanged over the quarter. Our overall duration exposure increased slightly over the quarter, more so internationally than in the U.S. We still shun Japan. We continue to prefer curve risk in rates to outright duration. Within commodities, we continue to run little directional risk with a slight bias to long energy over the quarter and we did add precious metals positions throughout the quarter. Within currencies, the dollar has continued to stand out as an effective trade for us at this point in the cycle.

Performance Drivers

The active components of the portfolio were the key drivers of performance over the quarter, in aggregate adding 0.8% toward portfolio performance. Fixed income directional and relative-value trades, foreign exchanges (FX), and commodities contributed positively. Systematic strategies detracted slightly in the fourth quarter (-0.3%), though exposure to alternatives contributed positively (+0.8%) toward performance. The main detractor from the portfolio was the core beta portfolio (strategic asset allocation), with both equities and bonds having a challenging quarter, detracting 2.8%.

Overlays

Within the systematic strategies,cross-asset trend and commodity mean reversion both detracted 0.15%.

Trend was hampered by the strong reversal in rates over the quarter (-0.9%), while FX contributed strongly, particularly in December (+0.8% over the quarter). The German 10-year bund was the main detractor while the Canadian dollar was the main contributor. Commodity mean reversion detracted 0.17%. Cocoa was the main detractor over the quarter, while exposure to natural gas and sugar added to performance. The Tactical Asset Allocation overlay added 0.7%. Both directional and relative-value trades added to portfolio performance.

*Includes equity index, interest rate/bond & commodity futures, as well as currencies and other derivatives. Weights are subject to change and may have changed since the date specified.

Source: Allspring Global Investments.

Portfolio Positioning: Underlying Funds

Equity funds were generally flat against their benchmarks over the quarter. Within fixed income, the Income Plus Fund delivered positive alpha against its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, while the Short-Term High Income Fund was flat. Exposure to the Alternative Risk Premia Fund added 0.4% toward portfolio performance over the quarter.

Outlook

Monetary policy in the U.S. has continued to get looser, though the Fed likely will take a more cautious approach given the still-elevated core inflation readings and the prospect of more Trump-induced fiscal stimulus to propel U.S. economic growth higher.

Real gross domestic product growth in the U.S. is likely to rise from 2.5% to around 3%, though medium-term growth is likely to moderate back to trend growth of around 2.5% as fiscal stimulus is already very loose and the marginal impact might not be as big as under the first Trump administration. The eurozone is dragged down by France and Germany, with Germany probably already in recession and consumer sentiment as well as manufacturing very weak. Equity earnings momentum continued to slow in the fourth quarter, though it is likely to pick back up later in 2025, driven by lower interest rates and continued fiscal stimulus as well as a robust U.S. consumer sentiment. International earnings are likely to remain volatile and majorly affected by potential U.S. trade tariffs. On the other hand, more fiscal stimulus from China should support international investor sentiment. Globally, bonds should continue to benefit from further rate cuts, although, short term, a lot of optimism is currently priced; the short-term interest rate market is expecting another two cuts in 2025, though if real yields remain elevated, we might see more rate cuts. European central banks are expected to cut much more aggressively this year, and China likely also needs to provide more stimulus to turn consumer sentiment around. Risks remain to the downside, like higher rates if the labor market begins to accelerate in the

data.title: Allspring Ultra Short-Term Income Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Resilient services activity backed by strong U.S. consumption continued to drive the U.S. economic expansion forward in the fourth quarter of 2024. The U.S. consumer remained healthy and continued to benefit from elevated income growth, low layoffs, and strong positive wealth effects. The housing and manufacturing sectors remained in mild recessions due to still-elevated financing rates, but sentiment indicators began to trend stronger post the U.S. election results. Labor market worries generally eased despite underlying labor market momentum continuing to gradually slow, with hiring slowing, long-term unemployment increasing, and U-3 unemployment rate ending the quarter just below post-COVID cycle highs. The pace of disinflation slowed, which left year-over-year rates at modestly elevated levels.

The Federal Reserve (Fed) continued its easing program and cut the federal funds rate by a total of 50 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) in the fourth quarter. Developed market central banks were largely parallel with the Fed and continued their easing programs in the fourth quarter with the Central Bank of Brazil and Bank of Japan remaining the key outliers that maintained their bias to hike. Global growth excluding U.S. was generally weak, which was supportive of gradual disinflation, with Chinese economic activity lackluster and stuck in secular deflation.

Current month-end performance is available at the fund’s website, allspringglobal.com. Institutional Class shares are sold without a front-end sales charge or contingent deferred sales charge.

The fund’s gross expense ratio is 0.37%. The fund’s net expense ratio is 0.26%. The manager has contractually committed, through December 31, 2025, to waive fees and/or reimburse expenses to the extent necessary to cap the fund’s total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers at 0.25% for the Institutional Class. Brokerage commissions, stamp duty fees, interest, taxes, acquired fund fees and expenses (if any), and extraordinary expenses are excluded from the expense cap. Prior to or after the commitment expiration date, the cap maybe increased or the commitment to maintain the cap maybe terminated only with the approval of the Board of Trustees. Without this cap, the fund’s returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning: Portfolio Attribution* and Strategy

Duration and yield curve—detractor

Duration and curve positioning detracted from performance during the quarter. The portfolio began the period positioned neutral relative to our strategic neutral, which remains long duration relative to the benchmark. Throughout the quarter, as Treasury yields rose, duration was extended. The fund had out-of-benchmark exposures to the 2- and 3-year portion of the yield curve where yields rose during the quarter while rates on the very front end fell.

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) cut the federal funds rate by 25 bps at both the November and December FOMC meetings. Rates rose across the yield curve throughout the quarter as investors adjusted their expectations for the path of rates in 2025. We expect that credit conditions will remain healthy, though we expect volatility to pick up in 2025. We also see two-way risk to the Fed’s dual mandates of price stability and full employment, complicating the path for rates. The current outlook suggests rates will remain higher for longer. We are carefully watching macro data and will adjust our duration bias based on how the data evolves. Global monetary policy trends will remain a headwind requiring close scrutiny, and as always, we will remain vigilant in our focus on risk given today’s unusual macro and market dynamics.

Quality—contributor Credit-quality allocations contributed to performance. An underweight to AA-rated bonds and overweights to A-rated and BBB-rated debt contributed, as did allocations to high yield sectors. An overweight to AAA-rated debt slightly detracted. Credit spreads traded in low, narrow ranges throughout much of the quarter, with some sectors such as U.S. investment-grade and high yield bonds setting their tightest levels in 20 years during the period. Lower-rated bonds outperformed higher-rated bonds during the quarter. Corporate fundamentals remain healthy, and the election raised expectations that pro-business policies could lower regulatory burdens and support the cycle. Continued strong demand for credit will likely keep spreads rangebound. Overall, we maintain a lower allocation to credit and continue to lean modestly up in quality across the portfolio as even modest spread widening could detract from excess returns for credit.

Sector—contributor

The fund’s overweight to corporate debt and exposures to below-investment-grade securities contributed to performance in the fourth quarter. Also adding to performance were the fund’s underweight to U.S. Treasuries and the out-of-benchmark allocation to securitized sectors. The fund’s allocation to non-agency commercial mortgage-backed securities, asset-backed securities (ABS), and collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) were the largest contributors, as spreads in these subsectors tightened during the period. Non-agency collateralized mortgage obligation (CMO) allocations were neutral to performance.

The fund’s allocation to securitized sectors increased 1% during the quarter as we modestly increased our exposure to ABS. The fund’s largest securitized exposure continues to be ABS, followed by non-agency CMOs and AAA CLOs.

Issue selection—contributor

Issue selection contributed to performance. Within credit, selection in technology, midstream, and automotive contributed. Selection in names like Renesas Electronics Corp. (OTCPK:RNECF), Kroger Co. (KR), Emera Inc. (OTCPK:EMRAF), and South Bow Corp. (SOBO) contributed. Selection in banking, metals and mining, and retailers detracted from performance. Selection in names like Wells Fargo & Co. (WFC), Bank of America (BAC), and Citigroup (C) detracted. We believe rate and spread volatility will persist. We have built optionality into the portfolio and have moved modestly up in quality. We will look to tactically exploit opportunities through timely adjustments to positioning using multiple levers based on our six-month outlook and unbiased approach.

*Performance attribution is discussed gross of expenses. The gross-of-expenses performance attribution does not reflect the deduction of the fund’s expenses as shown in the prospectus.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Allspring Short-Term Bond Plus Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Resilient services activity backed by strong U.S. consumption continued to drive the U.S. economic expansion forward in the fourth quarter of 2024. The U.S. consumer remained healthy and continued to benefit from elevated income growth, low layoffs, and strong positive wealth effects. The housing and manufacturing sectors remained in mild recessions due to still-elevated financing rates, but sentiment indicators began to trend stronger post the U.S. election results. Labor market worries generally eased despite underlying labor market momentum continuing to gradually slow, with hiring slowing, long-term unemployment increasing, and U-3 unemployment rate ending the quarter just below post-COVID cycle highs. The pace of disinflation slowed, which left year-over-year rates at modestly elevated levels.

The Federal Reserve (Fed) continued its easing program and cut the federal funds rate by a total of 50 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) in the fourth quarter. Developed market central banks were largely parallel with the Fed and continued their easing programs in the fourth quarter with the Central Bank of Brazil and Bank of Japan remaining the key outliers that maintained their bias to hike. Global growth excluding U.S. was generally weak, which was supportive of gradual disinflation, with Chinese economic activity lackluster and stuck in secular deflation.

Brokerage commissions, stamp duty fees, interest, taxes, acquired fund fees and expenses (if any), and extraordinary expenses are excluded from the expense cap. Prior to or after the commitment expiration date, the cap maybe increased or the commitment to maintain the cap maybe terminated only with the approval of the Board of Trustees. Without this cap, the fund’s returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning: Portfolio Attribution* and Strategy

Duration and yield curve—detractor neutral

Duration detracted from performance during the quarter. The portfolio was positioned close to our strategic neutral throughout the period, which is longer than the benchmark. Curve positioning also detracted from performance during the quarter. An overweight to the 6-month segment of the curve contributed, as did an underweight to the 1- and 3-year portions of the curve. This was more than offset, however, by out-of-benchmark allocations beyond 3 years. The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) cut the federal funds rate by 25 bps at both the November and December FOMC meetings. Rates rose across the yield curve throughout the quarter as investors adjusted their expectations for the path of rates in 2025. We expect that credit conditions will remain healthy, though we expect volatility to pick up in 2025. We also see two-way risk to the Fed’s dual mandates of price stability and full employment, complicating the path for rates. The current outlook suggests rates will remain higher for longer. We are carefully watching macro data and will adjust our duration bias based on how the data evolves. Global monetary policy trends will remain a headwind requiring close scrutiny, and as always, we will remain vigilant in our focus on risk given today’s unusual macro and market dynamics.

Quality—contributor

Credit-quality allocations contributed to performance. An underweight to AA-rated debt along with overweights to A-rated and BBB-rated debt added to performance, as did allocations to high yield sectors. An overweight to AAA-rated debt detracted from performance. Credit spreads traded in low, narrow ranges throughout much of the quarter, with some sectors such as U.S. investment-grade and high yield bonds setting their tightest levels in 20 years during the period. Lower-rated bonds outperformed higher-rated bonds during the quarter. Corporate fundamentals remain healthy, and the election raised expectations that pro-business policies could lower regulatory burdens and support the cycle. Continued strong demand for credit will likely keep spreads rangebound. Overall, we maintain a lower allocation to credit and continue to lean modestly up in quality across the portfolio as even modest spread widening could detract from excess returns for credit.

Sector—contributor

Sector allocation contributed to performance. Overweights to corporate credit and securitized sectors and an underweight to U.S. Treasuries contributed to performance. Within securitized, the fund’s out-of-benchmark allocations to asset-backed securities (ABS), commercial mortgage-backed securities, and collateralized loan obligations were the largest contributors. Allocations to non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities and agency mortgage-backed securities ('MBS') contributed to a lesser degree. Out-of-benchmark plus sector exposures to U.S. high yield bonds and loans contributed. European investment-grade and high yield allocations, emerging market debt, and global government bonds detracted from performance.

We remain overweight U.S. securitized exposures, within our neutral range for U.S. investment-grade corporates, and modestly underweight U.S. Treasuries. Within U.S. securitized, we are overweight agency MBS due to limited supply and attractive valuations while also finding opportunities in several ABS subsectors. Throughout the quarter, we modestly shifted our plus sector exposures. We slightly reduced our exposure to liquid global government bonds as the relative yield to U.S. Treasuries worsened. We added small additional exposures to U.S. and European high yield, and we increased exposure to European investment-grade credit given its relative-value advantage over its U.S. dollar (USDOLLAR,DXY) counterparts. Lastly, we have continued our neutral emerging market positioning, with South African local government bonds being our highest-conviction position.

Issue selection—contributor

Issue selection had a positive impact on performance. Within credit, selection in banking, finance companies, metals and mining, and midstream contributed. Selection in names like UBS Group (UBS); The Kroger Co. (KR); Emera Inc. (OTCPK:EMRAF); and Advance Auto Parts, Inc. (AAP) contributed. Selection in retailers, health insurance, and electric power detracted from performance. Selection in names like Citigroup Inc. (C) and the Asian Development Bank detracted. We believe rate volatility will persist and that credit spreads at current levels do not fully compensate investors. We have built optionality into the portfolio and have reduced lower-quality exposures with a focus on high-quality income. We will look to tactically exploit opportunities through timely adjustments to positioning using multiple levers based on our six-month outlook and unbiased approach.

*Performance attribution is discussed gross of expenses. The gross-of-expenses performance attribution does not reflect the deduction of the fund’s expenses as shown in the prospectus.

Original Post

data.title: Allspring Short Duration Government Bond Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

The Bloomberg U.S. 1–3 Year Government Bond Index fell 0.09% for the fourth quarter of 2024. Front-end U.S. Treasury yields were higher as the market scaled back rate-cut expectations through 2025 and 2026. The 2s/5s curve turned positive in the fourth quarter, steepening by 23 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) and ended the year at 13 bps.

The 2-year U.S. Treasury yields (US2Y) rose by 60 bps to end the fourth quarter at 4.24%, up from 3.64% where it ended in the third quarter and nearly flat to where it began the year. The 2s/10s curve continued to steepen in the fourth quarter and ended at 33 bps, steepening 19 bps throughout the quarter.

Market review

The U.S. economy continues to grow around its longer-term trend rate entering 2025, though there are signs that momentum is slowing. Manufacturing surveys remain at low levels and services surveys have been resilient, yet they have been mainly attributed to seasonal demand. Producers and services providers are reporting soft-order backlogs as buyers align to lower inventories. Inflation has improved and should continue to come down, though the process has been slower and more volatile than previously thought. Prices in core services categories accelerated in September and October before cooling somewhat in November. Labor costs remain a concern, having accelerated in recent reports, though they could improve given slowing job growth. Policymakers have embarked on a rate-cutting cycle, noting progress on inflation and a slowing jobs market. The Federal Reserve’s (Fed’s) decision in December to cut interest rates by 25 bps a range from 4.25% to 4.50% highlighting that policy is on a path toward normalization. Nonetheless, the Fed is likely to proceed more cautiously with growth and inflation still trending at or above recent projections. Markets responded positively to the U.S. election, which provided near-term certainty. Treasury Secretary nominee Scott Bessent is proposing a “3-3-3” economic plan: increasing potential economic growth to 3% by encouraging private sector investment, lowering the U.S. fiscal deficit to 3% of gross domestic product by 2028 by restraining government spending, and increasing domestic oil production by 3 million barrels per day. Tariffs could be an additional tool to rebalance trade and achieve foreign policy goals.

Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted and assumes the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current month-end performance is available at the fund’s website, allspringglobal.com. Institutional Class shares are sold without a front-end sales charge or contingent deferred sales charge.

The fund’s gross expense ratio is 0.54%. The fund’s net expense ratio is 0.42%. The manager has contractually committed, through December 31, 2025, to waive fees and/or reimburse expenses to the extent necessary to cap the fund’s total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers at 0.42% for the Institutional Class. Brokerage commissions, stamp duty fees, interest, taxes, acquired fund fees and expenses (if any), and extraordinary expenses are excluded from the expense cap. Prior to or after the commitment expiration date, the cap maybe increased or the commitment to maintain the cap maybe terminated only with the approval of the Board of Trustees. Without this cap, the fund’s returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning: Portfolio Attribution* and Strategy

Security selection—agency mortgages

The exposure to agency mortgages was a slight net detractor this quarter. Positioning in hybrid adjustable-rate mortgages (ARMs) was a contributor this quarter as the seasoned discount securities we own managed to grind tighter. Hybrid ARMs remain the largest contributor to performance by sector this year. Positioning in collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs) and passthroughs detracted modestly as the increase in volatility this quarter raised convexity hedging costs for both sectors.

Security selection—securitized

Asset-backed securities (ABS) and commercial mortgage-backed securities (''CMBS) were roughly equal contributors to performance in the aggregate during the fourth quarter. Credit card and auto dealer floorplan ABS and single-asset/single-borrower CMBS were the largest contributors. Non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities and private credit student loans detracted during the quarter.

*Performance attribution is discussed gross of expenses. The gross-of-expenses performance attribution does not reflect the deduction of the fund’s expenses as shown in the prospectus.

Original Post

data.title: Allspring Government Securities Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Resilient services activity backed by strong U.S. consumption continued to drive the U.S. economic expansion forward in the fourth quarter of 2024. The U.S. consumer remained healthy and continued to benefit from elevated income growth, low layoffs, and strong positive wealth effects. The housing and manufacturing sectors remained in mild recessions due to still-elevated financing rates, but sentiment indicators began to trend stronger post the U.S. election results. Labor market worries generally eased despite underlying labor market momentum continuing to gradually slow, with hiring slowing, long-term unemployment increasing, and U-3 unemployment rate ending the quarter just below post-COVID cycle highs. The pace of disinflation slowed, which left year-over-year rates at modestly elevated levels.

The Federal Reserve (Fed) continued its easing program and cut the federal funds rate by a total of 50 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) in the fourth quarter. Developed market central banks were largely parallel with the Fed and continued their easing programs in the fourth quarter with the Central Bank of Brazil and Bank of Japan remaining the key outliers that maintained their bias to hike. Global growth excluding U.S. was generally weak, which was supportive of gradual disinflation, with Chinese economic activity lackluster and stuck in secular deflation.

Investment return, principal value, and yields of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, maybe worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted and assumes the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current month-end performance is available at the fund’s website, allspringglobal.com. Institutional Class shares are sold without a

front-end sales charge or contingent deferred sales charge.

The fund’s gross expense ratio is 0.59%. The fund’s net expense ratio is 0.48%. The manager has contractually committed, through December 31, 2025, to waive fees and/or reimburse expenses to the extent necessary to cap the fund’s total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers at 0.48% for the Institutional Class. Brokerage commissions, stamp duty fees, interest, taxes, acquired fund fees and expenses (if any), and extraordinary expenses are excluded from the expense cap. Prior to or after the commitment expiration date, the cap maybe increased or the commitment to maintain the cap maybe terminated only with the approval of the Board of Trustees. Without this cap, the fund’s returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning: Portfolio Attribution* and Strategy

Duration and yield curve—contributor

Duration exposure had a neutral impact while curve positioning contributed to performance during the quarter. The portfolio was positioned neutral relative to the benchmark throughout most of the period. We also favored a curve steepening bias with overweights in the 2- and 5-year maturities and an underweight to the longer end, which added to performance. The FOMC cut the federal funds rate by 25 bps at both the November and December FOMC meetings. Rates rose across the yield curve throughout the quarter as investors adjusted their expectations for the path of rates in 2025. We expect that credit conditions will remain healthy, though we expect volatility to pick up in 2025. We also see two-way risk to the Fed’s dual mandates of price stability and full employment, complicating the path for rates. The current outlook suggests rates will remain higher for longer. We are carefully watching macro data and will adjust our duration bias based on how the data evolves. Global monetary policy trends will remain a headwind requiring close scrutiny, and as always, we will remain vigilant in our focus on risk given today’s unusual macro and market dynamics.

Quality—neutral

Given the fund’s high-quality mandate, credit-quality allocation rarely will be a contributor or detractor versus the benchmark. Sector—detractor An overweight to securitized sectors detracted from performance during the quarter. The fund’s overweight to agency mortgage-backed securities ('MBS') was the biggest detractor within securitized sectors as the MBS index delivered negative excess returns during the quarter. Also detracting from performance was exposure to agency collateralized mortgage obligations, where spreads widened during the quarter. The fund’s overweight to commercial mortgage-backed securities ('CMBS'), primarily agency CMBS, was the largest contributor within securitized sectors as spreads tightened during the period. The fund’s overweight to agency securities in lieu of U.S. Treasuries contributed to performance.

The fund’s overall securitized allocation declined 2% over the period, primarily driven by a reduction in our overweight to MBS.

Issue selection—neutral

Issue selection had a neutral impact on performance. Positioning within MBS contributed to performance, offsetting some of the underperformance at the sector level. Within MBS, we remain overweight production coupons (4.5% to 6.5%) as well as higher-coupon GNMAs.

*Performance attribution is discussed gross of expenses. The gross-of-expenses performance attribution does not reflect the deduction of the fund’s expenses as shown in the prospectus.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Allspring Core Plus Bond Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Resilient services activity backed by strong U.S. consumption continued to drive the U.S. economic expansion forward in the fourth quarter of 2024. The U.S. consumer remained healthy and continued to benefit from elevated income growth, low layoffs, and strong positive wealth effects. The housing and manufacturing sectors remained in mild recessions due to still-elevated financing rates, but sentiment indicators began to trend stronger post the U.S. election results. Labor market worries generally eased despite underlying labor market momentum continuing to gradually slow, with hiring slowing, long-term unemployment increasing, and U-3 unemployment rate ending the quarter just below post-COVID cycle highs. The pace of disinflation slowed, which left year-over-year rates at modestly elevated levels.

The Federal Reserve (Fed) continued its easing program and cut the federal funds rate by a total of 50 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) in the fourth quarter. Developed market central banks were largely parallel with the Fed and continued their easing programs in the fourth quarter with the Central Bank of Brazil and Bank of Japan remaining the key outliers that maintained their bias to hike. Global growth excluding U.S. was generally weak, which was supportive of gradual disinflation, with Chinese economic activity lackluster and stuck in secular deflation.

Investment return, principal value, and yields of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, maybe worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted and assumes the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current month-end performance is available at the fund’s website, allspringglobal.com. Institutional Class shares are sold without a front-end sales charge or contingent deferred sales charge.

The fund’s gross expense ratio is 0.49%. The fund’s net expense ratio is 0.35%. The manager has contractually committed, through December 31, 2025, to waive fees and/or reimburse expenses to the extent necessary to cap the fund’s total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers at 0.35% for the Institutional Class. Brokerage commissions, stamp duty fees, interest, taxes, acquired fund fees and expenses (if any), and extraordinary expenses are excluded from the expense cap. Prior to or after the commitment expiration date, the cap maybe increased or the commitment to maintain the cap maybe terminated only with the approval of the Board of Trustees. Without this cap, the fund’s returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning: Portfolio Attribution* and Strategy

Duration and yield curve—contributor

Duration exposure had a neutral impact while curve positioning contributed to performance during the quarter. The portfolio was positioned neutral relative to the benchmark throughout most of the period. We also favored a curve-steepening bias with overweights in the 2- and 5-year maturities and an underweight to the longer end, which added to performance. The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) cut the federal funds rate by 25 bps at both the November and December FOMC meetings. Rates rose across the yield curve throughout the quarter as investors adjusted their expectations for the path of rates in 2025. We expect that credit conditions will remain healthy, though we expect volatility to pick up in 2025. We also see two-way risk to the Fed’s dual mandates of price stability and full employment, complicating the path for rates. The current outlook suggests rates will remain higher for longer. We are carefully watching macro data and will adjust our duration bias based on how the data evolves. Global monetary policy trends will remain a headwind requiring close scrutiny, and as always, we will remain vigilant in our focus on risk given today’s unusual macro and market dynamics.

Quality—contributor

Credit-quality allocations contributed to performance. An overweight to AAA-rated debt contributed as did an underweight to AA-rated bonds and overweights to A- and BBB-rated debt along with allocations to high yield sectors. Credit spreads traded in low, narrow ranges throughout much of the quarter with some sectors such as U.S. investment-grade and high yield bonds setting their tightest levels in 20 years during the period. Lower-rated bonds outperformed higher-rated bonds during the quarter. Corporate fundamentals remain healthy, and the election raised expectations that pro-business policies could lower regulatory burdens and support the cycle. Continued strong demand for credit will likely keep spreads rangebound. Overall, we maintain a lower allocation to credit and continue to lean modestly up in quality across the portfolio as even modest spread widening could detract from excess returns for credit.

Sector—contributor

Sector allocation contributed to performance. Overweights to corporate credit and securitized sectors and an underweight to U.S. Treasuries contributed to performance. Within securitized, the fund’s out-of-benchmark overweight to asset-backed securities (ABS) was the largest contributor while overweights to commercial mortgage-backed securities, collateralized loan obligations, and non-agency collateralized mortgage obligations contributed to a lesser extent. The fund’s overweight to agency mortgage-backed securities ('MBS') was a small detractor, with the MBS Index delivering negative excess returns during the period, though this underperformance was more than offset by overweights to higher-coupon GNMAs and an underweight to lower-coupon MBS. Out-of-benchmark plus sector exposures to U.S. high yield bonds and loans contributed to performance. European investment-grade and high yield allocations, emerging market debt, and global government bonds detracted from performance.

We maintained our U.S. securitized exposure, increased our Treasury exposure, and modestly reduced the U.S. investment-grade credit allocation during the quarter. Within U.S. securitized, we are overweight agency MBS due to limited supply and attractive valuations while also finding opportunities in several ABS subsectors.

Throughout the quarter, we modestly shifted our plus sector exposures. We slightly decreased our exposure to global government bonds as the relative yield to U.S. Treasuries worsened. We trimmed U.S. high yield allocations as spreads narrowed to their tightest levels in two decades and modestly added to European high yield bonds that had underperformed their U.S. counterparts. We very slightly reduced exposure to European investment grade and trimmed exposure to emerging market debt. South African local government bonds remain our highest-conviction position there, offering attractive yields and adding diversification. Non-U.S. Dollar currency exposure was also reduced ahead of the U.S. election.

Issue selection—contributor

Issue selection had a positive impact on performance. Within credit, selection in banking, property and casualty, and pharmaceuticals contributed. Selection in names like UBS Group (UBS), Apache Corp., Warner Bros. Discovery Inc. (WBD), and debt from the Republic of South Africa contributed. Selection in retailers and airlines detracted from performance. Selection in names like Citigroup, Inc. (C); Pacific Gas & Electric Co. (PCG); and Panamanian Government International detracted. For the quarter, selection in higher-coupon Uniform Mortgage-Backed Securities and Government National Mortgage Association (OTCQB:FNMA, Ginnie Mae) coupons contributed to performance. We believe rate volatility will persist and that credit spreads at current levels do not fully compensate investors. We have built optionality into the portfolio and have reduced lower-quality exposures with a focus on high-quality income. We will look to tactically exploit opportunities through timely adjustments to positioning using multiple levers based on our six-month outlook and unbiased approach.

*Performance attribution is discussed gross of expenses. The gross-of-expenses performance attribution does not reflect the deduction of the fund’s expenses as shown in the prospectus.

Original Post

data.title: LNG Shipping Report 2024: Market Trends, Challenges, And Future Outlook
data.description:
Introduction

Last year was very difficult and different for the LNG shipping sector. In this report, we cover the main changes in the first section, show the yearly performance of all publicly listed LNG shippers, mention crucial events, and consider the future.

New normal?

The UP World LNG Shipping Index (UPI) tracks publicly listed global LNG shipping companies. In 2023, it ended at new all-time highs, and the LNG shipping sector was forecast to continue its traditional pattern of winter growth, spring decline, and summer growth, which intensifies towards the end of the year. However, this time, several factors were different. The main themes of LNG shipping in 2024 were oversupply and spot rates. The chart shows the development of UPI and the S&P 500.

Crucial changes in 2024

The UP World LNG Shipping Index (UPI) includes data from 2020 onwards. Last year was considered atypical, prompting us to create a simple moving average of the yearly trend based on weekly percentage gains or losses. The UPI gains and losses are also represented in percentage terms.

Previous years have not been typical either. The COVID-19 pandemic significantly impacted the index in 2020, followed by the Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022 and the preparations leading up to it in the prior year.

This report calculated a moving average of UPI data from 2020 to 2023 to better understand the differences over the past year.

Here are the four key sections:

  1. This negative trend continued throughout the last two months of the year, only to be surpassed at the end of the year and in January.
  2. Conversely, the latter half of the summer experienced a significant weakening of the UPI, establishing a negative trend for many companies throughout the remainder of the year.
  3. In June, as preparations for winter commence, the index typically rises. Notably, this peak was absent last year.
  4. The start of the year performed worse than expected as the peak was much lower than usual. However, the decline in March was less severe. During this period, reports of falling spot rates emerged and have persisted. The second quarter saw numerous declines in the UPI, coinciding with the end of the winter season and the onset of regular dry docks.

Based on this year's data, we theorise there are now two smaller peaks—one in winter and another in summer—rather than a single peak.

With older steam vessels being phased out, the LNG shipping sector is entering a new phase, prioritising efficiency over simple capacity expansion.

Constituents'performance

UPI consists of nineteen global LNG shipping companies from the U.S.A., Europe, and Asia. All constituents are named with tickers in the table with their 2024, Q4-24 and January 2025 gains or losses. The table is from the top 2024 gainers to the bottom. Some UPI constituents are not listed on U.S. exchanges.

Dividends are an essential part of investing, and shipping companies know this. UPI's yearly performance was +4.41%.

Name (Ticker)

Performance Q1-Q4 2024

New Fortess Energy Inc. (NFE)

(Data: UP-Indices.com)

Top gainers

Excelerate Energy started rising in February 2024 and still holds its top prices. This FSRU specialist and gas seller announced its Q4-23 results and the $50 million repurchase program on February 28th, 2024.

“Excelerate Energy delivered a powerful year of financial results in 2023. The consistent earnings contribution from our core regasification business and the solid performance of our contracts in Brazil highlighted the unique potential of our integrated strategy,” said Steven Kobos, President and Chief Executive Officer of Excelerate.

Dynagas LNG Partners rose since the end of March and, in two rising waves, got above $5.40. The rise started a week before Q4-23 earnings releases. During this event, DLNG announced the refinancing of its debt: „Signed a term sheet with a significant financial leasing operator in Asia for the lease financing of four of our six LNG carriers in an amount of up to $345.0 million.“ (source: Q4 and 2023 results announcement). The terms of the previous debt, which started in 2019, forbid the partnership from paying any distribution. After the refinancing, the first distribution was announced for the third quarter of 2024. DLNG operates six LNG carriers, three steam and three TFDE. All vessels are under a long-term charter till 2028. This shows a paradox of current spot rates – even an old vessel is competitive with modern ships as long as it has a long-term contract.

Golar LNG rose in mid-March after the company had gone sideways for a year. This initial rise was not accompanied by specific news but followed the Q4-23 results released on February 29th. FLNG Gimi has announced readiness to start operations. In September, a new impulse—or a rumour of a takeover—pushed the price above $40.

Top decliners

Two of the most dropping companies are affected by the spot rates. Cool Company holds part of its fleet on the spot market, and Awilco has one of its two ships on the spot market after finishing the time charter.

New Fortress Energy started its decline in March and accelerated it in July. Both waves were started by concern about new financing. An offering of $500 million of senior secure notes was announced in March. A day later, the amount was upsized to $750 million, and a tender offer was also announced to purchase cash for up to $250,000,000 in aggregate principal amount of its outstanding 6.750% Senior Secured Notes due 2025. In July, the closing of a new $700 million loan was announced, just after successfully producing LNG at its first FLNG unit. This step led to a significant drop in shares and later to changes in top management. In October, it also announced steps to debt and equity transactions to decrease the company liquidity, which started an uptrend lasting till now.

Awilco LNG has done well since March. The refinancing was signed in May, but in June, the long-term charter of WilForce was cancelled, and since then, the carrier has been traded on the spot market. Previous TCE was above $120,000 daily, one of the highest LNG shippers. Later, the dividend for Q3 was reduced to NOK0.25 from 0.75; for Q3 and Q4, it was not declared.

Jon Skule Storheill, Chief Executive Officer, commented on the Q3-24 press release: “After almost two years with robust earnings where both our vessels have been employed on well-paying Time Charter contracts, the Company has experienced a significant drop in earnings in the third quarter. WilForce is trading in the weakest LNGC spot market experienced for years; considering this is normally high season, it is surprisingly weak. The Company’s financial position is robust, with a comfortable cash position and lowered cash break even following the recent refinancing. Due to the present weakness and lack of visibility of near-term earnings, the board resolved to halt dividend payments until earnings are improved.”

Cool Company started to rise in May, similar to the UPI. A new long-term contract for a new build was announced, and the rise lasted till the end of May when earnings for Q2 were released. Here, the first decline of TCE from $87,300 to $77,200. This was still a standard value, but the development of rates was not promising, and investors started to look at the future rise of vessels without a long-term contract. This accelerated after Q3 results, where TCE was back at $81,600, but the dividend was reduced to $0.15 from $0.41, and a new build, Kool Tiger, was repositioned in the Atlantic basin for spot rates. A share repurchase program of up to $40 million was also announced.

2024 overview

This overview is based on our weekly reports and shows that last year was again full of unpredictable events, including geopolitical challenges, spot and interest rates, and investor preferences.

First quarter

In January, geopolitical problems started immediately on a vital shipping lane. Rising tension in the Red Sea and warm winter affected LNG shippers and the U.S. response to Houthis attacks in Jordan. Nearly all vessels had to sail around the Cape of Good Hope for security reasons.February brought the early end of the winter season. Due to low demand and high supplies, LNG shipping shares and the price of natural gas were pushed down. Henry Hub was currently close to its minimum of 2020. The spot rates of LNG shipping continued declining. The problem was that spot rates did not grow significantly above $100kpd during the year's peak period, so their decline was faster than usual. However, the longer route around the Cape of Good Hope partly offset this.March and April brought low volatility of UPI. This was mainly due to the off-season on the global LNG market. The three new trends of UPI constituents were 1) investors' confidence in the LNG market represented by a rise of Qatar´s Nakilat, 2) the growth of gas and oil drillers, and 3) ending the uptrend of the Japanese trio NYK Line, Mitsui O.S.K. Lines and „K“ Line.

Second quarter

A positive development in May was that Asian states continued to take advantage of low natural gas prices and spot rates, leading to an early season, and UPI went close to all-time highs again.

In June, the demand for LNG remained strong, mainly in Asia, but UPI experienced a significant drop of 4.25% in mid-June, closing at 155.45 points, while the S&P 500 index gained 1.58%. Despite most charters being long-term, spot rates still negatively affected the sector.

Third quarter

In July, spot rates rose due to Sparks Commodities: Atlantic rates rose to $87,000 per day, while Pacific rates rose by $4,000 to $51,750.

The positive sentiment on LNG shipping stocks pushed UPI to a new all-time high with a strong move but only average traded volume. One of the gainers in summer was Golar LNG, which rose 7% following a major 20-year deal with Argentina's Pan American Energy. New Fortress Energy was also successful, with a gain of over 20% in one week, driven by its FLNG Altamira LNG project launch. That was the last rise of NFE before the fall, which continued until November.

A Louisiana district court preliminarily cancelled the moratorium on new U.S. LNG export projects. This move can boost confidence in the LNG sector even though a sufficient number of projects have been permitted before.

August brought a global sell-off, but it was only a short period. Yet, in August, UPI made a new all-time high. The traded volume was under average.

Capital Product Partners LP has rebranded as Capital Clean Energy Carriers, with a new ticker symbol CCEC, and is also listed on NASDAQ.

At the end of August, another sell-off pushed UPI and global stock markets down. There was a trendline for UPI, and UPI was able to bounce up again to new all-time highs. This trendline broke in October, which meant a change in trend, and UPI lost for the rest of the year.

In September, rumours of the potential takeover of Golar LNG appeared, which pushed its shares up.

Fourth quarter

Spot rate challenges intensified in October as the expected pre-winter surge failed to materialise, leading to continued declines. According to a weekly summary by Marwa Rashad from Reuters, there has been a resurgence in LNG spot demand from Asian customers, while European storage levels remain high at 95% capacity. According to Spark Commodities, the spot rates are approximately $20,000 per day for the Atlantic and around $36,000 per day for the Pacific, below break-even levels. US LNG prices have dropped back below $3.

Q3 results: Oystein Kalleklev, CEO of FLEX LNG, stated the rates: "The spot market is liquid, but rates are poor, and we expect the market to stay poor for the remainder of the year." According to him, the low rates are caused by the excess of available ships: "You might have floating storage if gas prices are in contango, meaning that they are higher later in the year than spot, which can typically drive up to 30, 40 ships in floating storage. We have high gas prices this year, but they are not in contango. So it means you are disincentivised to do floating of the cargoes. So the number of available ships have been built up, also with the scheduled deliveries of ships."

The fear that low spot rates will affect dividend payments has also materialised. This inconvenience befell Awilco LNG (OSE: ALNG), which cancelled its Q3 dividend due to low rates. Its TCE fell from $122,900 in Q2 to $58,000 in Q3.

Low rates will rejuvenate the world's fleet, with newly delivered ships displacing inefficient old ones. After the long-term contract expires, steam-powered ships are no longer worth operating.

Lucy Hine from TradeWinds wrote in her article, "South Korean owner SK Shipping has sold four LNG carriers for demolition in an unprecedented multi-ship move that sends a clear signal to other players considering the future of their elderly steam turbine vessels."

It is essential for the future of LNG shipping that most of the ships that are ordered already have a pre-negotiated long-term contract. And it's not just Qatar Energy's mega-order.

New Fortress Energy led with a 13.6% gain after the Q3 earnings report, including bond refinancing news and progress in projects across Nicaragua, Puerto Rico, and Brazil. Excelerate Energy saw a 10% increase, backed by a significant dividend hike and fleet expansion plans. Although LNG shipping faces challenges like low spot rates, it remains critical to the global economy, with several companies experiencing substantial gains.

Winter's slow start, typically the sector's peak season, continues to impact the market. This was evident during Norwegian Cool Company’s Q3 earnings call, where executives highlighted concerns over spot rates. Despite achieving a solid TCE of $81,600, the lack of long-term contracts remains a significant challenge. Additionally, the recent dividend cut further underscores the sector's pessimistic outlook.

On the other hand, cooling in Asia is increasing the demand for natural gas and its price. This growth was more substantial than the commodity price increase for producers. US gas prices (Henry Hub) reached $3,276, an almost 5% week-on-week increase.

In December, Gas prices started to rise again, especially in Europe, and the first tankers were being diverted from Asia to Europe. Further growth may see tankers used as floating storage (contango), reducing or reversing the pressure on spot rates.

Global markets remained relatively stable despite geopolitical turbulence—including South Korea's brief martial law and Chinese manoeuvres near Taiwan.

The UPI recovered during the shortened Christmas week, rising prices above previously broken support levels. However, trading activity was lower than usual and mainly consisted of corrections to previous declines of index constituents.

Meanwhile, cold weather was still driving up the price of natural gas. However, the increased demand has not yet led to a rise in spot rates for LNG tankers. On a related note, Ukraine recently received its first U.S. LNG shipment through Greek ports, while the Russian LNG tanker Pioneer returned from a four-month unsuccessful mission to Asia. The end of the year brought sufficient growth, and the UPI strengthened for the second week in a row.

Winter has arrived in the northern hemisphere, and the pressure on gas and LNG prices continues. Ukraine has stopped sending Russian gas to Europe, which only brings grief to countries that neglected to prepare. But so far, these changes have not translated into spot rate changes. However, at least some investors have taken advantage of the low share prices and the absence of most investors, pushing stock prices up significantly.

Future of LNG shipping

What is the future of LNG shipping? In a word, optimistic. LNG is now a crucial global commodity, and its importance is still rising. The third wave of development is now running, but the construction of new liquefied capacities is a bit late. On the other hand, the shipbuilders are on time, so the vessel is oversupplied now.

The vessel oversupply is shown on the Flex LNG Q4 presentation chart.

This demonstrates the maturity of the LNG sector. Before the US's second wave of LNG development, this sector was considered new and risky for infrastructure investors due to uncertain demand. That perception has changed; new standards and economic factors are now critical.

The third wave of development is now running, and the near rise of capacity in Qatar and other states will add about 200 MTPA. Shipping companies prepare with newbuilds for this supply (and demand) rise. Steam LNG carriers with old long-term contracts - the publicly listed ones are mainly Japanese shippers, pioneers of LNG - are now taking work away from modern vessels. Modern carriers without long-term charters are effective, but now, there is only a small space for them. As soon the charters of the steamships are finished, steamers will be uncompetitive. The last picture, again from the Flex LNG Q4 presentation, shows the evolution of steam vessel rates.

The scrapping of steam vessels has already begun, but the pace is still slow. This year may be challenging for vessels depending on spot rates; however, if there are no significant geopolitical changes, we can expect improvements by the end of the year. The new U.S. administration is promoting natural gas drilling, which is making the global market more competitive. As LNG prices decrease, demand will rise, benefiting LNG shippers.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Allspring Core Bond Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index fell 3.06% for the fourth quarter of 2024. U.S. Treasury yields across the curve were significantly higher as the market continued to push back rate-cut expectations and ultimately move to price in a higher neutral rate.

10-year U.S. Treasury (US10Y) yields rose by 79 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) to end the year at 4.57%, up from 3.88% where it began the year. The 2s/10s curve continued to steepen, ending at 33 bps, steepening 29 bps throughout the quarter. The option-adjusted spread on the Bloomberg U.S. Credit Bond Index tightened by 7 bps to 77 bps over U.S. Treasuries, 16 bps tighter from +93 bps ending 2023.

Market review

The U.S. economy continues to grow around its longer-term trend rate entering 2025, though there are signs that momentum is slowing. Manufacturing surveys remain at low levels and services surveys have been resilient, yet they have been mainly attributed to seasonal demand. Producers and services providers are reporting soft-order backlogs as buyers align to lower inventories. Inflation has improved and should continue to come down, though the process has been slower and more volatile than previously thought. Prices in core services categories accelerated in September and October before cooling somewhat in November. Labor costs remain a concern, having accelerated in recent reports, though they could improve given slowing job growth. Policymakers have embarked on a rate-cutting cycle, noting progress on inflation and a slowing jobs market. The Federal Reserve’s (Fed’s) decision in December to cut interest rates by 25 bps to 4.50% highlights that policy is on a path toward normalization. Nonetheless, the Fed is likely to proceed more cautiously, with growth and inflation still trending at or above recent projections. Markets responded positively to the U.S. election, which provided near-term certainty. Treasury Secretary nominee Scott Bessent is proposing a “3-3-3” economic plan: increasing potential economic growth to 3% by encouraging private sector investment, lowering the U.S. fiscal deficit to 3% of gross domestic product by 2028 by restraining government spending, and increasing domestic oil production by 3 million barrels per day. Tariffs could be an additional tool to rebalance trade and achieve foreign policy goals.

Figures quoted represent past performance, which is noguarantee of future results, and donotreflect taxes a shareholder maypay on an investment in a fund. Investment return, principal value, and yields of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, maybe worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted and assumes the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current month-end performance is available at the fund’s website, allspringglobal.com. Institutional Class shares are sold without a front-end sales charge or contingent deferred sales charge.

The fund’s gross expense ratio is 0.48%. The fund’s net expense ratio is 0.38%. The manager has contractually committed, through August 31, 2025, to waive fees and/or reimburse expenses to the extent necessary to cap the fund’s total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers at 0.38% for the Institutional Class. Brokerage commissions, stamp duty fees, interest, taxes, acquired fund fees and expenses (if any) from funds in which the underlying master portfolios and funds invest and from money market funds, and extraordinary expenses are excluded from the expense cap. All other acquired fund fees and expenses from the affiliated master portfolios and funds are included in the expense cap. Prior to or after the commitment expiration date, the cap may be increased or the commitment to maintain the cap may be terminated only with the approval of the Board of Trustees. Without this cap, the fund’s returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning: Portfolio Attribution* and Strategy

Security selection—credit

Security selection in credit was a positive contributor to performance in the quarter, led by positioning across the communications, technology, and U.S. banks subsectors. In communications, overweights to Charter (CHTR), Crown Castle (CCI), META, and Comcast (CMCSA) led contributions. In technology, overweights to Broadcom (AVGO), Accenture (ACN), and Cadence (CDNS) led contributions. In U.S. banks, an overweight to Santander USA (SAN) as well as an underweight to JPMorgan (JPM) led contributions. Away from the above subsectors, an underweight to Panama (sovereigns) and an overweight to PG&E (PCG, utilities) also made notable contributions. While security selection in credit was a leading contributor to performance, positioning across the insurance and foreign agency subsectors did lead detractors in the quarter. In insurance, detractors were modest and broad based, driven by an underweight to the subsector. In foreign agencies, an overweight to Galaxy Pipeline and Gospodarstwa Krajowego detracted. Away from the above subsectors, overweights to Mexico (sovereigns), Pfizer (PFE), and HCA (health care) also detracted.

Security selection—agency mortgages

Agency mortgages contributed to performance this quarter, with positioning and security selection driving the performance.

Security selection in collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs) has been the largest contributor, followed by select passthrough cohorts. Spreads on both fixed- and floating-rate CMOs have held in exceptionally well against elevated rate volatility and index-level spread volatility. In passthroughs, the underweight to discount coupon 30-years has contributed, as did the overweight to higher-coupon loan balance pools. The small position in hybrid adjustable-rate mortgages has also been a modest contributor as well. The positive performance was partly offset by modest detraction from the sector overweight, as mortgage spreads struggled in the elevated volatility environment this quarter. The overweight to lower-coupon 20-years and seasoned 30-year 3.0s also detracted modestly, as both coupons widened in the move.

Security selection—securitized

During the fourth quarter, both our sector overweight to asset-backed securities (ABS) and security selection within the ABS sector contributed, with CUSIP-specific contributions across device payment plan, auto loan, and auto dealer floorplan ABS sectors. Holdings of certain non-qualified mortgage residential mortgage-backed securities, private credit student loan ABS, and credit card ABS were detractors on a risk-adjusted basis. Within the commercial mortgage-backed securities sector, our underweight detracted while security selection contributed, specifically from single-asset/single-borrower and ’24 vintage 5-year conduit positions.

*Performance attribution is discussed gross of expenses. The gross-of-expenses performance attribution does not reflect the deduction of the fund’s expenses as shown in the prospectus.

Original Post
